analogy: Think of "Role of the Government in Health" through the chapter's own opening images: 10 Social and Political Life Chapter 1: On Equality UNIT TWO 11 Teachers note These two chapters (Chapters 2 and 3) on State Government are an attempt to discuss the functions and structure of government through concrete situations. We have chosen health as an example;... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Role of the Government in Health is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that 10 Social and Political Life Chapter 1: On Equality UNIT TWO

The text explains that 11 Teachers note These two chapters (Chapters 2 and 3) on State Government are an attempt to discuss the functions and structure of government through concrete situations.

The text explains that we have chosen health as an example; there could have been other, equally important, choices.

The text explains that chapter 2 discusses health as an important issue for people.

The text explains that there are both public and private aspects of health provisioning.

The text explains that while the Constitution supports a view that the right to health is an aspect of our fundamental rights, its provisioning is rather unequal.

The text explains that through the accounts provided, learners will begin to visualise the ideal or desired role of government, and the meanings behind its structures.

The text explains that some ways in which this situation can be changed are also discussed.

The text explains that chapter 3 focuses on how the government functions, and discusses ideas of representation, accountability and public welfare.

The text explains that though both the executive and the legislature are presented, one should not expect students to retain fine distinctions.

The text explains that it would be best to patiently encourage them to ask questions such as, Who is the most powerful person?, Why cant the MLA solve the problem?, etc.

The text explains that such queries will enable them to construct a sense of the government apparatus.

The text explains that it is important that learners acquire the confidence to express their views on public issues and understand the role of government through the exercises given in the chapters.

The text explains that you could choose familiar issues such as water, transport, school- fees, books, child-labour, etc., for them to discuss and arrive at how these problems need to be tackled.

The text explains that allow them to express these ideas through wall charts.

The text explains that given that discussions on the government and its functioning often lead to boredom and cynicism, we need to be able to make the classroom session less didactic and more interactive while teaching these lessons.

The text explains that also, the resources needed to run these services are obtained from the money that we, the public, pay to the government as taxes.

The text explains that tax money is utilised for relief and rehabilitation in case of natural disasters such as floods, earthquakes, tsunami etc.

The text explains that this includes schools, hospitals, telephone services, etc.

The text explains that people can demand these services and also raise questions about their non-functioning.

The text explains that private: An activity or service that is organised by an individual or company for their own gain.

The text explains that medical tourists: This refers to foreigners who come to this country specifically for medical treatment at hospitals that offer worldclass facilities at a lower cost than what they would have to pay in their own countries.

The text explains that communicable diseases: These are diseases that are spread from one person to another in many ways such as through water, food , air, etc.

The text explains that oPD: This is the short form for Out Patient Department.

The text explains that this is where people are first brought in and treated in a hospital without being admitted to any special ward.

The text explains that ethics: Moral principles that influence a persons behaviour Generic names: These are chemical names of the drugs.

The text explains that for example, acetyl salicylic acid is the generic name of Aspirin.

The text explains that facility Affordability Availability Quality Private Public Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) www.in.undp.org

Source: NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II, Chapter 2: Role of the Government in Health

prelims_traps: Do not treat 10 Social and Political Life Chapter 1: On Equality and 2CHAPTER as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Role of the Government in Health; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Role of the Government in Health through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 10 Social and Political Life Chapter 1: On Equality and 2CHAPTER to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
